q: What is an AI/BI Genie space in Databricks, and how does a business user interact with it?
a: A Genie space is a domain-scoped natural-language chat interface where business users ask questions in plain English and get back generated SQL, a results table, and visualizations. An analyst curates the space with a set of Unity Catalog tables, then refines it with column comments, example SQL queries, and text instructions. Genie generates SQL that runs against the underlying tables, so users see real, governed data, not a hallucinated answer. It is built for the analyst-to-business handoff, not for engineers writing SQL by hand.
fj: Genie stores its own copy of the data and answers from a trained model.
fs: Genie generates SQL that runs live against your Unity Catalog tables — it doesn't hold a separate dataset or answer from model weights.

followups.q: If a user asks a question Genie can't answer confidently, what should it do rather than guess?
followups.a: Genie should surface the generated SQL and flag low confidence rather than silently return a number. A good space lets users inspect and edit the SQL, and the curator adds example queries and instructions so future similar questions resolve correctly. The anti-pattern is treating any returned table as authoritative — the generated SQL is the artifact to review, not just the result.

followups.q: How does Genie respect a user's data permissions when generating SQL?
followups.a: Genie runs the generated SQL as the requesting user under Unity Catalog, so it can only read tables and rows that user is granted. Row-level security and column masking policies on the underlying tables flow through automatically — a user without access to a masked column sees the mask in Genie's answer too. There's no separate AI permission layer to maintain.

followups.q: Can Genie answer questions that require joining several tables, and what does it need to do that well?
followups.a: Yes, but multi-table SQL is where it most often goes wrong. Defining join relationships (primary/foreign key hints and example join queries) in the space teaches Genie how to connect tables correctly. Without that, it may invent join keys or pick the wrong grain, so curating relationships and a few canonical example queries is the highest-leverage fix for multi-table accuracy.

q: What is Mosaic AI, at a high level? What does it bundle?
a: Mosaic AI is Databricks' end-to-end GenAI/ML stack layered on the lakehouse and governed by Unity Catalog. It bundles Model Serving (including Foundation Model APIs and external models), Vector Search for retrieval, the Agent Framework for building and deploying agents, Agent Evaluation with LLM judges, Model Training for fine-tuning, and MLflow for tracking, tracing, and the model registry. The pitch is that your data, embeddings, models, and governance all live in one platform instead of being stitched across separate vector DB, serving, and MLOps tools. Unity Catalog provides a single permission and lineage layer over all of it.
fj: Mosaic AI is just a hosted LLM endpoint.
fs: It's the whole GenAI stack — serving, vector search, agents, evaluation, training, and MLflow — unified under Unity Catalog governance.

followups.q: What is the single biggest architectural advantage of this bundling versus best-of-breed point tools?
followups.a: Shared governance and lineage. Because data, the Vector Search index, served models, and agents all register in Unity Catalog, one permission and lineage model covers everything — you can trace a model back to its training data and inherit access controls instead of reconciling separate ACL systems across a vector DB, a serving platform, and an MLOps tool.

followups.q: Where does Agent Bricks fit relative to the lower-level Mosaic AI components?
followups.a: Agent Bricks is the higher-level, declarative layer: you describe the task and provide data, and Databricks auto-builds, evaluates, and optimizes an agent on top of the same primitives — Vector Search, Model Serving, and Agent Evaluation. The Agent Framework remains the code-first path when you need full control; Agent Bricks trades flexibility for speed on common patterns.

followups.q: Does adopting Mosaic AI lock you into Databricks-hosted models only?
followups.a: No. Foundation Model APIs serve Databricks-hosted open models, but external models let you register OpenAI, Anthropic, or other providers behind the same governed endpoint, and you can serve your own fine-tuned or custom models. The unifying layer is Unity Catalog and the AI Gateway, not a single model vendor.

q: What is a Delta Sync index in Mosaic AI Vector Search and why is it the default choice?
a: A Delta Sync index is a vector index built from a Delta table that automatically stays in sync as the source table changes. You point it at a column and Databricks keeps the embeddings updated as rows are inserted, updated, or deleted, so you never run a separate re-embedding pipeline. It can either compute embeddings for you from a text column using a managed embedding model, or sync a column where you supply your own vectors. This is preferred because it eliminates the drift between your governed source-of-truth Delta table and your retrieval index.
fj: You must manually re-embed and re-upload documents whenever the source data changes.
fs: A Delta Sync index auto-updates embeddings as the underlying Delta table changes — no manual re-ingestion pipeline.

followups.q: What's the difference between pipeline_type TRIGGERED and CONTINUOUS, and how do you choose?
followups.a: TRIGGERED syncs on demand and then stops, so you pay only while a refresh runs — best for periodic batch updates where minutes of staleness are fine. CONTINUOUS keeps a streaming pipeline running to propagate changes within seconds, at the cost of always-on compute. Pick TRIGGERED for most RAG corpora and CONTINUOUS only when near-real-time freshness justifies the standing cost.

followups.q: What's the trade-off between letting Databricks compute embeddings (embedding_source_column) versus syncing your own vectors?
followups.a: With `embedding_source_column` plus an `embedding_model_endpoint_name`, Databricks manages embedding on every change — simplest, and the model stays consistent. Self-managed embeddings (`embedding_vector_column`) give you full control over the model and chunking but mean you own re-embedding whenever you change models. Use managed unless you need a specific embedding model or custom preprocessing the platform can't express.

followups.q: Your source table updates constantly but the index seems stale — what would you check first?
followups.a: If `pipeline_type` is TRIGGERED, the index only updates when a sync is triggered, so 'stale' is expected between runs — check the sync schedule or trigger a refresh. Also confirm Change Data Feed is enabled on the source Delta table, since Delta Sync relies on it to detect incremental changes; without CDF the sync can fall back to slow full reprocessing.

q: A business user complains Genie keeps misunderstanding 'active customer' and returns wrong numbers. How do you improve Genie's accuracy?
a: Genie's accuracy comes from the metadata and assets you give it, in order of leverage. First, make sure table and column descriptions in Unity Catalog are clear, since Genie reads those plus sample values to infer meaning. Then add example SQL queries that show how to solve common questions, and define SQL expressions for business terms like 'active customer' so the definition is unambiguous. Use free-text instructions only as a last resort for rules that the other assets can't express. Defining join relationships also teaches Genie how to author multi-table SQL correctly.
fj: You should retrain or fine-tune the underlying model on the company's data.
fs: You don't train Genie — you curate it: column comments, example queries, SQL expressions for business terms, and instructions.

followups.q: Why is a SQL expression for 'active customer' better than just writing it in free-text instructions?
followups.a: A SQL expression is executable and unambiguous — Genie substitutes the exact filter (e.g. `last_order_date > current_date - 90`) into generated queries every time. Free-text instructions are interpreted by the LLM and can be applied inconsistently or ignored under paraphrasing. Encode anything that has one correct definition as an expression; reserve instructions for soft guidance the other assets can't capture.

followups.q: How would you measure whether your curation actually improved Genie before rolling it out broadly?
followups.a: Build a benchmark of representative questions with known-correct answers and run them through the space, comparing generated SQL and results against ground truth. Genie supports curated example/verified queries and a question-set workflow, so you can regression-test changes. Treat curation like code: a labeled eval set turns 'it feels better' into a measurable accuracy delta.

followups.q: When is the right answer to tell the business user Genie shouldn't answer a given question at all?
followups.a: When the question depends on data not in the space, on logic that can't be expressed in SQL over the curated tables, or on metrics with contested definitions across teams. Forcing Genie to answer those produces confident-but-wrong numbers. Scoping the space tightly and documenting what's out of bounds is more trustworthy than over-broad coverage.

q: Foundation Model APIs offer pay-per-token and provisioned throughput. When do you pick each?
a: Pay-per-token bills per input/output token with no standing endpoint cost, so it's ideal for development, prototyping, spiky traffic, and low-volume production where you don't want to pay for idle capacity. Provisioned throughput reserves a guaranteed token-per-second capacity on a dedicated endpoint, which Databricks recommends for all serious production workloads needing predictable latency and throughput, and it's required to serve your own fine-tuned models. The tradeoff is the usual serverless-versus-reserved economics: pay-per-token is cheaper when idle but can be more expensive and rate-limited at high sustained volume.
fj: Provisioned throughput is always cheaper, so use it everywhere.
fs: Pay-per-token wins for spiky/low-volume and dev; provisioned throughput wins for sustained high-volume production and serving fine-tuned models.

code.label: Query a pay-per-token FM API
lines: from openai import OpenAI  # FM API

followups.q: Pay-per-token endpoints enforce rate limits. What happens to a high-throughput batch job and how do you handle it?
followups.a: Pay-per-token has per-workspace token and query limits, so a large batch will hit `429` throttling and slow to a crawl. For sustained batch or high-QPS serving, move to provisioned throughput, which reserves a guaranteed tokens-per-second band. Short term you can add backoff/retry, but the structural fix is reserved capacity sized to the workload.

followups.q: How is provisioned throughput capacity actually sized, and what's the risk of under- or over-provisioning?
followups.a: You allocate capacity in tokens-per-second bands per endpoint, varying by model architecture. Under-provision and requests queue or get throttled, raising latency under load; over-provision and you pay for idle reserved capacity around the clock. You size from measured peak token rate plus headroom, and revisit as traffic grows.

followups.q: Why can't you serve a fine-tuned foundation model on pay-per-token?
followups.a: Pay-per-token endpoints serve a shared pool of Databricks-hosted base models, so there's no dedicated capacity to host your custom weights. A fine-tuned or custom model needs its own optimized serving endpoint, which is what provisioned throughput provides — dedicated inference capacity for that specific model architecture and weights.

q: What are external models in Databricks Model Serving, and why route OpenAI or Anthropic through them instead of calling the provider directly?
a: External models let you register a third-party provider like OpenAI, Anthropic, or a cloud LLM behind a Databricks serving endpoint, so your apps query it with the same unified, OpenAI-compatible API as your in-house models. The point is centralized governance: you manage provider credentials once, and every call flows through Mosaic AI Gateway where you get permissions, rate limits, payload logging, usage tracking, and guardrails. That means you can swap providers without touching app code, enforce PII and safety filters uniformly, and get one audit trail across all your LLM spend instead of scattered direct API keys.
fj: There's no reason to proxy a provider — just call the OpenAI API directly from the app.
fs: Routing through external models gives one governed endpoint: shared credentials, rate limits, logging, guardrails, and provider swap without app changes.

followups.q: Where are the provider API keys stored when you register an external model, and why does that matter?
followups.a: Credentials are stored as Databricks secrets and referenced by the endpoint config, not embedded in app code. That means keys are managed and rotated centrally, individual apps never see them, and you avoid the sprawl of direct keys checked into notebooks or env files. Revoking or rotating a provider key is one change at the endpoint, not a hunt across every app.

followups.q: What does provider fallback on an external-model endpoint actually buy you?
followups.a: The AI Gateway can route traffic to a backup model/provider if the primary errors or is unavailable, so a provider outage degrades gracefully instead of failing user requests. It also lets you shift load for cost or capacity reasons. The caller keeps using one stable endpoint while the routing decision happens server-side.

followups.q: What latency or feature trade-offs come from proxying a provider through Databricks?
followups.a: You add a network hop and gateway processing (guardrails, logging), which is small but non-zero, and the unified OpenAI-compatible schema may not expose every bleeding-edge provider-specific parameter immediately. You accept slight overhead and a normalized API in exchange for governance, logging, and swap-ability — usually a good trade for enterprise apps but worth noting for ultra-latency-sensitive paths.

q: What does the ai_query SQL function do, and when would you reach for a task-specific AI Function instead?
a: ai_query is the general-purpose AI Function that lets you invoke any serving endpoint — a Foundation Model API, an external model, or your own custom model — directly from SQL or Python, with full control over the model, prompt, and parameters. You'd use it to apply an LLM across a whole table, like classifying or summarizing every row in a batch. Task-specific functions like ai_classify, ai_extract, ai_summarize, and ai_translate wrap common tasks with tuned prompts so you don't hand-write the instruction. Reach for the task-specific one when it fits the job; drop to ai_query when you need a custom prompt, a specific model, or structured output control.
fj: You need a Python notebook and a serving client to apply an LLM to a table.
fs: AI Functions run LLMs inline in SQL — ai_query for full control, ai_classify/ai_extract/etc. for common tasks.

followups.q: How do you get reliable structured output (e.g. JSON) out of ai_query instead of free-form text?
followups.a: `ai_query` accepts a `responseFormat` so you can pass a JSON schema and have the model return conforming structured output, which you then parse into typed columns. This is far more robust than prompting for JSON in free text and regexing the result. Use it whenever downstream SQL needs typed fields rather than a blob of prose.

followups.q: You run ai_query over a 50M-row table and it's slow and expensive. What levers do you pull?
followups.a: Don't score rows you don't need — filter first, and dedupe identical inputs. Right-size the model: a smaller or distilled model often suffices for classification, reserving the large model for hard cases. Back the endpoint with provisioned throughput so the batch isn't throttled, and batch in partitions. The biggest win is usually narrowing the input set before any LLM call.

followups.q: When would a task-specific function like ai_classify be the wrong choice even though it 'fits'?
followups.a: When you need a label set, prompt nuance, or output format the wrapper doesn't expose — e.g. multi-label with custom rationale, a specific model, or schema-constrained JSON. The task functions trade flexibility for convenience. The moment you're fighting the wrapper to get the behavior you want, drop to `ai_query` with an explicit prompt and `responseFormat`.

q: How does the vector_search SQL function let you do RAG retrieval without leaving SQL?
a: The vector_search() table function queries a Mosaic AI Vector Search index from SQL, returning the top-k most similar rows for a query string or vector. Combined with ai_query in the same statement, you can retrieve relevant chunks and feed them into an LLM prompt entirely in Databricks SQL, which makes a lightweight RAG pattern expressible as one query. Because the index is a Delta Sync index, the rows it returns are always backed by fresh, Unity Catalog-governed data. This is handy for analysts and for embedding retrieval inside Lakeflow pipelines without standing up a separate serving app.
fj: RAG retrieval always requires a Python application and a separate vector database client.
fs: vector_search() runs retrieval inside SQL against a governed Delta Sync index — you can even chain it into ai_query for inline RAG.

followups.q: When does inline SQL RAG hit its limits, pushing you to a proper Agent Framework deployment?
followups.a: SQL RAG is great for single-shot retrieve-then-generate inside analytics or pipelines, but it can't do multi-step reasoning, tool calls, conversation memory, or per-request tracing and evaluation. The moment you need an agent that iterates, calls tools, or is monitored as a served endpoint, wrap the logic in an MLflow ChatAgent and deploy it instead of expressing it as one query.

followups.q: Can you apply metadata filters in vector_search to scope retrieval, and why does that matter?
followups.a: Yes — you can pass a filter on indexed columns (e.g. `filters` on tenant, document type, or date) so retrieval only considers permitted or relevant rows. This both improves precision and enforces scoping, like restricting a tenant's queries to its own documents. Filtering at retrieval is usually cheaper and safer than retrieving broadly and filtering after.

followups.q: How do governance and permissions apply when vector_search runs inside a SQL statement?
followups.a: The index is a Unity Catalog object and inherits the access controls of its source Delta table, so a user can only query an index they're granted, and the rows returned reflect the governed source of truth. Retrieval doesn't bypass governance — it rides on the same UC permission model as the underlying data.

q: What is hybrid search in Mosaic AI Vector Search and why does it often beat pure vector search?
a: Hybrid search runs an approximate-nearest-neighbor vector search and a keyword (full-text) search in parallel, then merges the two ranked lists with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Pure vector search captures semantic meaning but can miss exact tokens like product SKUs, error codes, or proper names, while keyword search nails those exact matches but misses paraphrases. Fusing them gives you both semantic recall and lexical precision, which is why hybrid is usually the safer default for enterprise retrieval where identifiers matter. On Databricks the keyword index is built from your data (an inverted index) and hybrid is available with no extra setup.
fj: Vector similarity alone is always the best retrieval method.
fs: Pure ANN misses exact tokens like SKUs and codes; hybrid fuses vector + keyword via RRF to get both semantic recall and lexical precision.

followups.q: Why use Reciprocal Rank Fusion to merge the two result lists instead of comparing raw scores?
followups.a: Vector cosine scores and keyword BM25-style scores live on different, non-comparable scales, so you can't just add them. RRF ignores absolute scores and combines by rank position (summing 1/(k+rank) across lists), which is robust and parameter-light. That makes fusion stable even when one retriever returns wildly different score magnitudes than the other.

followups.q: Give a concrete case where hybrid actually hurts and pure vector would be better.
followups.a: For purely conceptual queries with no important literal tokens — 'how do customers feel about onboarding' — the keyword arm can pull in lexically-matching but semantically-irrelevant rows and dilute strong semantic hits. When identifiers don't matter and paraphrase recall dominates, pure vector (or down-weighting the keyword arm) can be cleaner. Hybrid is the default, not a universal win.

followups.q: How does chunking interact with hybrid search quality?
followups.a: Both arms retrieve at chunk granularity, so chunks that split an identifier from its context, or that are too large and topic-mixed, hurt both lexical and semantic matching. Self-contained chunks that keep an entity and its describing text together improve keyword precision and embedding quality simultaneously. Chunking is often a bigger lever on retrieval quality than the search mode itself.

q: What is the Mosaic AI Agent Framework and how does ChatAgent fit in?
a: The Agent Framework is Databricks' supported path for building, evaluating, and deploying production GenAI agents that connect LLMs to your governed enterprise data via Vector Search, tools, and Model Serving. You author your agent — using any library like LangChain or LlamaIndex — and wrap it in MLflow's ChatAgent interface, implementing predict for non-streaming and predict_stream for streaming responses. Conforming to ChatAgent gives you out-of-the-box compatibility with the AI Playground, Agent Evaluation, the Review App for SME feedback, and Agent Monitoring. You then log the agent as an MLflow model and deploy it.
fj: An agent must be a custom REST service you build and host yourself.
fs: Author with any library, wrap in MLflow ChatAgent (predict/predict_stream), then log and deploy — you get Playground, Evaluation, and Monitoring for free.

followups.q: Why does implementing predict_stream matter, not just predict?
followups.a: `predict_stream` yields incremental chunks so the Playground, Review App, and your front end can render tokens as they arrive instead of waiting for the full answer — critical for perceived latency on long generations. `predict` is the non-streaming fallback. Implementing both lets the same agent serve streaming UIs and simple request/response callers without forking code.

followups.q: Why conform to the standard ChatAgent schema instead of inventing your own request/response shape?
followups.a: ChatAgent is a defined message schema, so conforming wires the agent into Databricks tooling for free — Playground chat, Agent Evaluation, the Review App, and monitoring all speak that schema. A custom shape means rebuilding all of that integration yourself. The interface is the contract that makes the rest of the lifecycle plug-and-play.

followups.q: Does using LangChain or LlamaIndex inside the agent change how you deploy it?
followups.a: No — the orchestration library is an implementation detail behind `predict`/`predict_stream`. As long as the agent conforms to ChatAgent and you log it (ideally Models-from-Code) with its resource dependencies declared, deployment via `agents.deploy()` is identical regardless of the library. The framework is library-agnostic by design.

q: Walk me through deploying an agent with the Agent Framework after you've built it.
a: You log the agent as an MLflow model — typically with Models-from-Code so the agent's source is captured — declaring its resources like the serving endpoint and Vector Search index so Unity Catalog can manage access. You register that logged model as a version in Unity Catalog, then call agents.deploy(), which provisions a Model Serving endpoint and spins up the Review App for subject-matter experts to chat with the agent and leave feedback. MLflow tracing is enabled on the endpoint so every request's retrieval and tool steps are captured. From there you iterate using evaluation results and production traces before promoting.
fj: You deploy an agent by manually building a Docker container and standing up your own API server.
fs: Log to MLflow, register in Unity Catalog, then agents.deploy() provisions the serving endpoint, Review App, and tracing automatically.

followups.q: Why must you declare resources (serving endpoint, Vector Search index) when logging the agent?
followups.a: Declaring resources lets Unity Catalog provision the right access for the deployed endpoint so the agent can reach its index and LLM at runtime without you hand-managing credentials. It also records dependencies for lineage and reproducibility. Skip it and the deployed agent may fail at request time because it can't authenticate to a resource it needs.

followups.q: Why is Models-from-Code preferred over pickling the agent object?
followups.a: Models-from-Code logs the agent's actual source file, so the model is reconstructed by re-running code rather than unpickling a serialized object graph. That sidesteps brittle pickle/version mismatches with complex libraries like LangChain and gives a readable, reviewable artifact. It's the more robust and reproducible way to capture agents.

followups.q: What's the role of the Review App between deploy and promote-to-production?
followups.a: The Review App gives subject-matter experts a chat UI to exercise the deployed agent and attach feedback/labels to specific responses, generating the human signal you fold into an evaluation set. Combined with production traces, it's how you decide whether a version is good enough to promote — closing the loop between deployment, expert review, and the next iteration.

q: What is Mosaic AI Agent Evaluation and how do its LLM judges work?
a: Agent Evaluation scores your agent's responses against a curated evaluation set using built-in LLM-as-a-judge graders, so you measure quality systematically before and after deployment instead of eyeballing outputs. Each judge checks one dimension — correctness, groundedness, relevance, safety — and emits a yes/no verdict plus a written rationale. A key distinction: correctness compares the answer to a reference answer and so needs ground-truth labels, while groundedness checks whether the response is supported by the retrieved context and needs no labels, which makes it usable in production monitoring. You can also tune or build custom judges to encode domain-specific quality rules.
fj: Evaluating an agent means a human manually reads every response.
fs: Built-in LLM judges score correctness, groundedness, relevance, and safety with rationales — groundedness needs no labels, so it works in live monitoring.

followups.q: Which built-in judges require ground-truth labels and which don't, and why does that split matter?
followups.a: Correctness needs a reference answer to compare against, so it requires labeled examples. Groundedness, relevance (retrieval and answer), and safety judge the response against the question and retrieved context, needing no labels. That split is why the label-free judges can run on live production traffic for monitoring, while correctness is confined to your offline labeled eval set.

followups.q: An LLM judge is itself a model — how do you trust its verdicts?
followups.a: You calibrate judges against human labels: have SMEs grade a sample, then check agreement with the judge and tune prompts or thresholds until they align. Each verdict ships with a rationale you can audit, and you can author custom judges for domain rules. The point is judges scale evaluation, but you validate them against human ground truth rather than trusting blindly.

followups.q: When would you write a custom judge instead of relying on the built-ins?
followups.a: When quality depends on domain rules the generic dimensions don't capture — e.g. 'must cite a policy number', 'must not give medical dosing advice', or a required tone/format. You define a custom judge (guidelines or a scorer) to encode that rule and run it alongside the built-ins. Built-ins cover general quality; custom judges encode what's specific to your product.

q: Your RAG agent gives answers that sound right but cite the wrong documents. Which judge catches this and how do you debug it?
a: Groundedness is the judge that catches this: it flags when the response isn't actually supported by the retrieved context, which is exactly the failure mode of a confident answer built on the wrong chunks. To debug, open the MLflow trace for failing examples and inspect the retrieval span to see what was actually retrieved — usually the problem is upstream in retrieval, not generation. Common fixes are switching to hybrid search so exact identifiers match, improving chunking so chunks are self-contained, raising num_results, or adding metadata filters. Separating the retrieval judge from the generation judge tells you which half of the pipeline to fix.
fj: If the answer reads well, the model is fine and there's nothing to debug.
fs: A fluent but wrong-source answer is a groundedness failure — inspect the retrieval span in the trace; the bug is usually retrieval, not generation.

followups.q: How do groundedness and retrieval-relevance judges localize the failure differently?
followups.a: Retrieval relevance scores whether the retrieved chunks even matched the question — a low score means retrieval pulled the wrong documents. Groundedness scores whether the answer is supported by whatever was retrieved. If retrieval relevance is high but groundedness is low, generation is ignoring good context; if retrieval relevance is low, fix retrieval first. The pair tells you which half to touch.

followups.q: Raising num_results is one suggested fix — when does that backfire?
followups.a: More chunks add noise and tokens: irrelevant context can distract the model and raise the chance it grounds on the wrong passage, while also increasing latency and cost. Beyond a point, recall gains plateau and precision drops. Often a reranker or better chunking beats simply retrieving more — increase `num_results` deliberately, not as a reflex.

followups.q: If retrieval is clearly correct but the model still cites the wrong source, what generation-side fixes apply?
followups.a: Tighten the prompt to instruct grounding only in provided context and to cite chunk IDs, reduce conflicting context, and consider a stronger or differently-tuned model. You can also add a post-hoc check that verifies cited spans appear in retrieved text. When retrieval is good and groundedness still fails, the fix moves to prompt design and model behavior, not the index.

q: What is the Mosaic AI / Unity AI Gateway and what controls does it centralize over LLM traffic?
a: The AI Gateway is the governance layer that sits in front of Model Serving and external-model endpoints, giving you one place to control all LLM traffic. It enforces permissions and rate limits per endpoint, logs request/response payloads and usage to Unity Catalog tables for audit and cost attribution, and applies AI Guardrails like PII detection, safety filtering, and topic/keyword restrictions on inputs and outputs. It also supports provider fallback so traffic can fail over between models. The benefit is you get security, observability, and cost controls across your whole AI estate without writing custom middleware in each app.
fj: Each application has to implement its own rate limiting, PII filtering, and usage logging.
fs: The AI Gateway centralizes rate limits, payload/usage logging to Unity Catalog, PII/safety guardrails, and provider fallback — no per-app middleware.

followups.q: Guardrails apply to both inputs and outputs — why do you need both directions?
followups.a: Input guardrails block or mask sensitive/disallowed content before it reaches the model (preventing PII leaking into a third-party provider, or off-topic prompts). Output guardrails catch harmful or PII-containing content the model generates before it reaches the user. A prompt can be clean while the completion is unsafe and vice versa, so enforcing only one side leaves a gap.

followups.q: What does routing payload and usage logging into Unity Catalog tables enable that app logs don't?
followups.a: Because usage lands in governed UC tables, you can query it with SQL for per-endpoint and per-team cost attribution, audit who asked what, and build dashboards/alerts — all under the same access controls as your data. Scattered application logs can't be uniformly queried, secured, or attributed; centralizing in UC turns LLM traffic into governed, analyzable data.

followups.q: Does putting the gateway in front of every call add meaningful latency, and when might you bypass guardrails?
followups.a: Guardrail and logging processing adds a small per-request cost, usually negligible relative to model inference time. For ultra-latency-critical internal paths with already-trusted input you might run lighter guardrails, but you rarely give up logging/rate-limits. The default is to keep traffic governed and accept the minor overhead for the security and observability payoff.

q: What is Mosaic AI Model Training (Foundation Model Fine-tuning) and when is fine-tuning the right call over RAG or prompting?
a: Mosaic AI Model Training lets you fine-tune open foundation models on your own data, registering the resulting model to Unity Catalog as a version you can then serve with provisioned throughput. The decision ladder: start with prompting and few-shot, add RAG when the model needs current or proprietary facts, and fine-tune when you need to change behavior — a consistent format, tone, domain style, or to distill a smaller cheaper model that matches a big one on your task. Fine-tuning is the wrong tool for injecting frequently changing facts, because retraining is slow and the knowledge goes stale; that's RAG's job.
fj: Fine-tuning is how you give a model your latest company data.
fs: Fine-tuning changes behavior/format/style, not facts — use RAG for current proprietary knowledge; fine-tune to shape outputs or distill a cheaper model.

followups.q: Can you combine fine-tuning and RAG, and when is that the right move?
followups.a: Yes — they're complementary. Fine-tune to lock in format, tone, or domain reasoning, and keep RAG to inject current proprietary facts at query time. A common pattern is a distilled, cheaper fine-tuned model that's also fed retrieved context. Use both when you need consistent behavior and fresh knowledge; neither alone covers both axes.

followups.q: What does distillation buy you economically, and what's the catch?
followups.a: Distilling a large model's behavior into a smaller fine-tuned model can match quality on your specific task at much lower per-token cost and latency, then serve it on provisioned throughput. The catch: the small model's competence is narrow — it holds up on the trained task distribution but degrades off-distribution, so you must guard the inputs and re-evaluate when the task shifts.

followups.q: Why does fine-tuning to inject facts cause 'stale knowledge', and what's the failure it produces?
followups.a: Fine-tuned facts are baked into weights at training time, so anything that changes after — prices, policies, inventory — is frozen and wrong, and updating means a slow, costly retrain. Worse, the model states the stale fact confidently with no signal it's outdated. RAG sidesteps this by reading current governed data at query time, so the same answer updates the instant the source table does.

q: Design an end-to-end production RAG application on Databricks. What components do you wire together?
a: Land and clean source documents into Delta tables (bronze to silver), chunk them, then build a Delta Sync Vector Search index so embeddings stay current with the data automatically. At query time the agent embeds the question, runs hybrid search against the index, assembles a prompt, and calls an LLM via a Model Serving endpoint — Foundation Model API or your fine-tuned model. Wrap the whole flow as an MLflow ChatAgent, log it, register to Unity Catalog, and deploy with agents.deploy(). Govern access through Unity Catalog, route LLM calls through the AI Gateway for guardrails and logging, score quality with Agent Evaluation, and watch production with MLflow tracing and Lakehouse Monitoring.
fj: You bolt a standalone vector DB and a hosted LLM onto a notebook and call it production.
fs: Production RAG on Databricks chains Delta source → auto-syncing Vector Search → served LLM → MLflow-logged agent, all governed by Unity Catalog with evaluation and tracing.

followups.q: Where in this pipeline are the failure points most likely to be, and how does the design surface them?
followups.a: Retrieval is the usual culprit — bad chunking or wrong search mode — followed by grounding failures in generation. The design surfaces them because MLflow tracing records each retrieval and LLM span per request, and Agent Evaluation's retrieval-relevance and groundedness judges localize whether retrieval or generation broke. You debug a specific failing span, not the whole agent.

followups.q: How do you safely roll out a new version of this RAG app without risking production?
followups.a: Register the new agent as a new Unity Catalog version, evaluate it offline against your labeled eval set, then deploy and exercise it via the Review App with SMEs. Compare evaluation metrics and production traces against the current version before promoting. UC versioning plus served endpoints lets you stage, A/B, or roll back rather than overwriting prod in place.

followups.q: What keeps this app's retrieval fresh as source documents change, without a separate re-embedding job?
followups.a: The Delta Sync Vector Search index. Because it's bound to the silver Delta table, inserts/updates/deletes propagate into the index automatically (TRIGGERED on a schedule or CONTINUOUS for near-real-time), so embeddings track the source of truth. You never stand up a parallel re-ingestion pipeline, which is the main source of index/source drift in DIY stacks.

q: A teammate wants to build RAG on Databricks with a separate Pinecone vector DB and a direct OpenAI key. Make the build-vs-buy case for staying native.
a: The external stack works, but it fragments your governance and lineage: documents live in Unity Catalog while embeddings live in Pinecone, so access policies, row/column masking, and audit don't flow through, and you now own a re-embedding pipeline to keep the two in sync. A direct OpenAI key bypasses central rate limits, PII guardrails, payload logging, and cost attribution. Native Mosaic AI keeps embeddings auto-synced to Delta via a Delta Sync index, inherits Unity Catalog permissions automatically, and routes the LLM through the AI Gateway for guardrails and logging. Choose external only when you need a specific model or capability the platform genuinely lacks — and accept you're rebuilding governance yourself.
fj: An external vector DB plus a direct provider key is simpler and just as governed.
fs: External stacks split your data from its governance and force a sync pipeline; native keeps embeddings, permissions, and LLM logging unified — go external only for a true capability gap.

followups.q: What's the strongest legitimate reason to still pick the external vector DB?
followups.a: A genuine capability gap: you need a feature Mosaic AI Vector Search doesn't offer — a specific ANN algorithm, an extreme scale/latency profile, multi-cloud portability, or an existing heavy investment in that stack. If the requirement is real and the platform can't meet it, external is justified — but you knowingly take on rebuilding governance, lineage, and sync yourself.

followups.q: Concretely, what governance breaks when embeddings live in Pinecone but documents live in Unity Catalog?
followups.a: UC row-level security and column masking apply to the Delta table but not to vectors copied into Pinecone, so a retrieval can surface content a user shouldn't see. Lineage from answer back to source data breaks, and audit/access policies don't span the boundary. You'd have to replicate masking and ACLs in the external store and keep them in sync — error-prone and easy to drift.

followups.q: How would you quantify the 'you now own a sync pipeline' cost to make the argument land?
followups.a: Frame it as ongoing engineering: a re-embedding job triggered on every source change, plus handling deletes/updates, backfills, schema changes, embedding-model upgrades, monitoring, and on-call for drift between source and index. A Delta Sync index makes all of that a managed config. The external choice trades a one-line index for a maintained data pipeline and its failure modes.

q: Compare Databricks Genie with Snowflake's Cortex Analyst as a text-to-SQL / conversational BI approach.
a: Both turn natural language into SQL that runs against the warehouse, but they differ on where the semantic model lives. Cortex Analyst uses a customer-authored YAML semantic model exposed as a REST endpoint — git-versionable and reviewable in PRs, which engineers like but analysts find less intuitive. Genie leans on Unity Catalog metadata — table/column comments, example queries, SQL expressions — so existing UC governance like row-level security and column masking flows into the AI automatically, with no parallel governance layer to maintain. Genie is native inside the Databricks notebook/SQL workflow; Cortex's YAML is more of an explicit, CI-friendly artifact. Pick based on which platform your data and governance already live on.
fj: They're the same product with different branding.
fs: Cortex Analyst = explicit git-versioned YAML semantic model; Genie = curated on Unity Catalog so UC governance flows in automatically. The difference is where the semantics and governance live.

followups.q: Snowflake now offers semantic views as native objects — how does that change the comparison?
followups.a: Semantic views move the semantic layer from a YAML file on a stage into a native, schema-level Snowflake object managed under RBAC and sharing, which Snowflake recommends over the legacy stage-based YAML for new builds. That narrows the gap: Cortex's semantics become a governed in-warehouse object rather than an external file, making it closer to Genie's UC-native model while keeping an explicit, queryable definition.

followups.q: Cortex Analyst's REST endpoint is a genuine advantage in some cases — when?
followups.a: When you want to embed conversational BI into your own application rather than use a vendor chat UI. The Cortex Analyst REST API returns generated SQL and supports multi-turn follow-ups, so you can build a custom front end against it. The explicit, versionable semantic model also fits teams that want the BI definition reviewed in PRs and tested in CI.

followups.q: What practical limit should you know about Cortex Analyst's semantic model?
followups.a: There's a size cap — the semantic model or view is limited to about 2 MB, and after sample values and verified queries are separated out the core definition can't exceed roughly 32K tokens. That forces you to keep the model focused and curated rather than dumping an entire warehouse schema into it, which is itself good practice but a real constraint on very large models.

q: What does MLflow Tracing give you for GenAI apps, and why is it different from logging?
a: MLflow Tracing captures the full execution of a GenAI request as a tree of spans — the prompt, each retrieval, every tool call, and intermediate LLM steps — with inputs, outputs, latency, and token usage at each step. Unlike flat logging, this structure lets you see exactly where an agent went wrong: which chunks were retrieved, what the model actually received, which tool failed. Traces are OpenTelemetry-compatible and feed Agent Evaluation and Lakehouse Monitoring, so the same traces you debug with in development power real-time quality scoring, latency/cost dashboards, and alerts in production. It's the observability backbone that turns 'the agent is wrong sometimes' into a specific failing span.
fj: A print/log line per request is enough to debug an agent.
fs: Tracing records the whole span tree — retrievals, tool calls, prompts, tokens, latency — so you find the exact failing step instead of guessing from flat logs.

followups.q: Why does OpenTelemetry compatibility matter for traces?
followups.a: OTel is an open standard, so MLflow traces can interoperate with existing observability tooling and you can instrument across services without a proprietary format locking you in. It also means consistent span semantics (parent/child, timing, attributes) that other systems understand. You get a portable, standards-based trace rather than a Databricks-only artifact.

followups.q: How do the same traces serve both dev debugging and production quality monitoring?
followups.a: In dev you open a trace to find the failing span; in production those traces are scored by label-free judges like groundedness and feed latency/cost dashboards and alerts. Because the structure is identical, you don't build a second observability stack for prod — the artifact you debug with is the artifact you monitor with, which keeps dev and prod signals consistent.

followups.q: What's the cost/overhead concern with full tracing at production scale, and how do you manage it?
followups.a: Capturing full inputs/outputs per span adds storage and some latency, and at high QPS the trace volume is significant. You manage it by sampling traces, trimming or redacting large payloads (also helps PII), and retaining detail on errors/low-quality responses while sampling the happy path. The aim is enough signal to debug and monitor without storing every byte of every request forever.

q: What is Feature Serving in Databricks and how does it relate to Model Serving for real-time inference?
a: Feature Serving exposes precomputed features and on-demand feature functions as a low-latency, auto-scaling REST endpoint, backed by an online store (Lakebase) that mirrors your offline Delta feature tables while preserving governance and lineage. You define a FeatureSpec in Unity Catalog — a reusable bundle of FeatureLookups and FeatureFunctions — and serve it. The relevance to Model Serving is that real-time inference often needs fresh features at request time: the model endpoint can look up features from the same FeatureSpec so the exact transformations used in training are reused at serving, eliminating online/offline training-serving skew. It keeps one definition of a feature across both worlds.
fj: You recompute features in your application code at inference time.
fs: A FeatureSpec defines features once and serves them from an online store, so training and serving use identical transforms — no training/serving skew.

followups.q: What is the difference between a FeatureLookup and a FeatureFunction in a FeatureSpec?
followups.a: A FeatureLookup retrieves a precomputed feature from a feature table by join keys — values computed ahead of time and served from the online store. A FeatureFunction computes a feature on-demand at request time, often from request inputs that can't be precomputed (e.g. distance from a just-supplied location). A FeatureSpec bundles both so one definition covers stored and freshly-computed features.

followups.q: Why is reusing the FeatureSpec at training and serving the thing that kills training/serving skew?
followups.a: Skew arises when the transformation that produced training features differs from what runs in production — a reimplemented function, a different default, a units mismatch. Pulling both training (`create_training_set`) and the serving endpoint from the same FeatureSpec means one definition of each feature, so the inference-time computation is provably the same code path as training. There's no second implementation to drift.

followups.q: What does Lakebase as the online store provide that querying the offline Delta table directly wouldn't?
followups.a: Lakebase is a low-latency, autoscaling store optimized for point lookups by key at high QPS, which the analytics-oriented offline Delta table can't serve within real-time latency budgets. It mirrors the offline tables while keeping governance and lineage, so you get millisecond feature reads for serving without sacrificing the consistency and access controls of the source feature tables.

q: How do you control and attribute the cost of a GenAI workload on Databricks?
a: First pick the right serving mode: pay-per-token for spiky or low-volume traffic so you pay nothing when idle, provisioned throughput only when sustained volume or latency guarantees justify reserved capacity. Use the AI Gateway to enforce rate limits per endpoint and to log usage to Unity Catalog, which gives you per-endpoint and per-team cost attribution instead of one opaque bill. Cut token spend by retrieving fewer, better chunks, caching, and routing easy requests to a smaller or distilled fine-tuned model while reserving the large model for hard ones. Then watch cost and latency in Lakehouse Monitoring dashboards and alert on spikes before they blow the budget.
fj: GenAI cost is fixed by the model you chose and can't really be managed.
fs: Manage cost with the right serving mode, gateway rate limits and usage logging for attribution, tighter retrieval/caching, model right-sizing, and monitoring alerts.

followups.q: How does the gateway's usage logging actually enable per-team cost attribution?
followups.a: The AI Gateway writes per-request usage (tokens, endpoint, identity) into Unity Catalog tables, so you can `GROUP BY` team, endpoint, or app to split the bill with SQL and feed it into dashboards. Without it, provider invoices are one aggregate number you can't decompose. Governed usage tables turn LLM spend into queryable, attributable data.

followups.q: Beyond model choice, what's the single biggest token-cost lever in a RAG app?
followups.a: Context size. Retrieving fewer, higher-quality chunks (better chunking, reranking, tighter `num_results`) cuts input tokens on every call, which usually dominates cost in RAG. Bloated context also hurts quality via distraction, so tightening retrieval often improves accuracy and cost simultaneously — a rare win-win lever.

followups.q: When does a model-routing / cascade strategy pay off, and what's its hidden cost?
followups.a: Routing easy requests to a small/distilled model and escalating only hard ones to the large model pays off when a large fraction of traffic is genuinely easy — you capture big savings. The hidden cost is the router itself (latency, an extra call or classifier) plus the risk of misrouting hard queries to the weak model. It's worth it at scale with a skewed difficulty distribution, less so for uniformly hard workloads.

q: Why is Unity Catalog central to governing AI on Databricks, not just data? Give concrete examples.
a: Unity Catalog is the single governance plane over data and AI assets, so the same permission and lineage model covers tables, Vector Search indexes, registered models, agents, functions, and feature specs. Concretely: a Delta Sync index inherits the access controls of its source table; a fine-tuned model is registered as a UC model version with full lineage back to its training data; Genie can only expose tables the user is permitted to query, and row/column policies flow into its answers; the AI Gateway logs LLM usage into UC tables for audit. This means you grant and audit AI access with the same primitives as data, and lineage lets you trace a model back to the exact data it learned from.
fj: AI models and indexes need their own separate permission system outside the catalog.
fs: Unity Catalog governs models, indexes, agents, and functions with the same permissions and lineage as tables — one plane, plus model-to-training-data lineage.

followups.q: Why does model-to-training-data lineage matter operationally, not just on paper?
followups.a: When a data quality issue, a deletion request, or a compliance question surfaces, lineage lets you identify exactly which models and indexes were built from the affected data and need retraining or re-indexing. Without it you're guessing at blast radius. It also supports audits — proving what a model learned from — which is increasingly required in regulated settings.

followups.q: Give a concrete example where a UC policy on a table automatically protects an AI feature downstream.
followups.a: If a column mask hides SSNs on a customer table, then Genie answering over that table returns masked values, and a Delta Sync index built from it inherits the source's access controls so retrieval can't surface restricted rows to an unauthorized user. The policy is defined once on the table and the AI surfaces honor it — no separate enforcement in the AI layer.

followups.q: What's the failure mode for governance when teams bypass UC with external stores or direct keys?
followups.a: Governance fragments: data copied to an external vector DB no longer carries UC masking/ACLs, direct provider keys skip gateway logging and rate limits, and lineage breaks at every boundary. You end up reimplementing permissions and audit in each external system and reconciling them by hand — exactly the sprawl UC exists to eliminate. The cost is real but invisible until an audit or breach exposes the gap.

q: What are Genie trusted assets and how does each type shape the SQL Genie generates?
a: Genie trusted assets are the curated inputs inside a Genie space that guide NL-to-SQL generation: (1) table and column descriptions from Unity Catalog metadata give vocabulary; (2) SQL expressions map business terms like 'active user' or 'MRR' to exact SQL fragments that get substituted verbatim; (3) example SQL queries show correct multi-table joins, date arithmetic, and filters for canonical questions; (4) free-text instructions encode editorial rules the other three can't express. The priority order matters: SQL expressions are the most reliable because they're executable and unambiguous; instructions are a last resort because LLM interpretation can be inconsistent. Adding a wrong example query is worse than no example, because Genie may over-fit to its pattern on related questions.
fj: Genie trusted assets are just the table list you attach to the space.
fs: Trusted assets = UC column comments + SQL expressions (verbatim substitution) + example queries + instructions. SQL expressions beat instructions for precision; examples set join/grain patterns.

followups.q: Why can a single badly authored example query cause broad regression in a Genie space?
followups.a: Genie uses example queries as anchors for similar NL patterns. An example that uses the wrong join key, the wrong date grain, or a non-canonical filter can be applied to structurally similar questions and produce wrong SQL across that whole class. You must validate examples against known-correct results before adding them, and benchmark the space before and after each addition.

followups.q: When would you choose a free-text instruction over a SQL expression for the same business rule?
followups.a: Only when the rule genuinely can't be encoded in SQL — for example, 'when the user asks about revenue, always break it down by region unless they say otherwise' is a presentation and framing preference with no single SQL mapping. SQL expressions handle deterministic filter/metric definitions; instructions handle soft formatting and framing conventions. Using instructions where an expression would work means accepting inconsistent application.

followups.q: How do you test that a new SQL expression doesn't break existing Genie answers?
followups.a: Maintain a Genie evaluation set — a fixed set of representative questions with verified SQL and expected results. Before and after adding the expression, run all questions and compare generated SQL and result tables. Any regression signals the expression is over-matching adjacent question patterns. Treat expressions like code: reviewed, tested, and rolled back if they break the eval set.

q: Explain the Genie Conversations API: what it exposes and how you'd use it to embed conversational BI in a custom application.
a: The Genie Conversations API is a REST API that lets you programmatically start and continue multi-turn conversations against a Genie space from outside the Databricks UI. You create a conversation (POST /genie/spaces/{space_id}/start-conversation), send follow-up messages (POST /genie/spaces/{space_id}/conversations/{conversation_id}/messages), and poll the message result for the generated SQL, query results, and any clarifying questions Genie raises. This lets you build a branded BI chat UI, a Slack bot, or any custom front end that surfaces governed analytics without exposing the Databricks workspace directly. Access still enforces Unity Catalog permissions — the API runs as the caller's identity, so row/column policies apply.
fj: Genie only works inside the Databricks SQL UI; there's no programmatic access.
fs: The Genie Conversations API exposes start-conversation + message endpoints so you can embed Genie multi-turn NL-to-SQL in any app while UC permissions still apply per the caller's identity.

code.label: Start a Genie conversation via REST

followups.q: The Genie API is asynchronous — what does that mean for how you poll a message result?
followups.a: After sending a message you get a message_id; you then GET the message result endpoint and check the status field, which transitions through EXECUTING_QUERY to COMPLETED or FAILED. You must poll until a terminal state, then parse the result_data. This async pattern exists because SQL generation and execution can take several seconds; building a synchronous wrapper around it requires an exponential-backoff polling loop or a webhook, not a simple blocking call.

followups.q: How does the Genie API handle follow-up questions that refer back to prior context in the conversation?
followups.a: Each message is posted to the same conversation_id, so Genie retains the prior turn's SQL and context — follow-ups like 'now break that down by region' resolve correctly because Genie knows what 'that' refers to. Conversational context is server-side; the client just passes the conversation_id and the new message text. If you start a new conversation for every question you lose multi-turn resolution.

followups.q: A Slack bot calls the Genie API and surfaces answers in a channel. What governance concerns arise?
followups.a: The bot authenticates with a service principal token, so Genie runs every query as that principal's UC permissions rather than the individual Slack user's. That can expose data the Slack user shouldn't see if the service principal has broad grants. The correct design is a per-user OAuth token or a pattern where the Slack user's identity is passed as the calling principal, not a broad service account, to preserve per-user RLS and column masking.

q: How does Genie handle NL-to-SQL governance in multi-tenant environments where different users should see different row subsets?
a: Genie runs the generated SQL as the requesting user's identity via Unity Catalog, so row-level security policies defined on the underlying Delta tables apply automatically at query execution. If user A's UC identity has a row filter that restricts to their tenant's rows, Genie's SQL runs with that filter enforced by the storage layer — Genie doesn't see a cross-tenant result set and then try to mask it. This means you define multi-tenancy once as UC policies on the tables, and Genie inherits them without any extra logic in the space. The critical implication: a shared Genie space can safely serve users from different tenants as long as the underlying tables have correct row filters.
fj: You must build a separate Genie space per tenant to avoid data leakage.
fs: Genie runs SQL as the caller's UC identity, so row-level security on the underlying tables enforces tenant isolation automatically — one space, many tenants, no leakage if UC policies are correct.

followups.q: What specific UC construct implements row-level filtering on a Delta table for Genie to inherit?
followups.a: Row filters are defined as Unity Catalog security policies via `ALTER TABLE ... SET ROW FILTER`, pointing to a SQL function that takes table column values and returns a boolean. Unity Catalog pushes the filter predicate at scan time, so any query — including Genie-generated SQL — runs with that predicate silently applied. You author the filter once on the table; all upstream consumers inherit it.

followups.q: If a Genie space is used by a service account running a scheduled report, what multi-tenant risk does that create?
followups.a: A service account typically has broader grants than an individual user, potentially spanning multiple tenants. A Genie query run as that account bypasses per-user row filters and could return cross-tenant rows. For scheduled or automated Genie use, the service account should have the same tight RLS scope as the business user it's acting for — or you must verify the service account's grants are explicitly constrained to the correct tenant.

followups.q: Can column masking policies — e.g. masking PII like email — also flow through Genie, and how?
followups.a: Yes. Column masking is defined via `ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET MASK` referencing a SQL masking function. When Genie generates a SELECT that includes the masked column and runs it as the user, UC applies the mask at scan time — the user sees the masked value, not the raw PII, exactly as if they ran the query in SQL editor directly. Genie doesn't special-case masking; it inherits it because it runs through the standard UC query path.

q: How do you benchmark a Genie space's NL-to-SQL accuracy, and what metrics matter?
a: Build an evaluation set of representative business questions paired with ground-truth SQL and verified result tables. Run every question through the Genie space and compare on three metrics: SQL correctness (does the generated SQL match the expected SQL's logic or at minimum produce the same result), result accuracy (do the numbers match the known-correct output), and coverage (what fraction of questions produce a valid SQL attempt vs. Genie declining). Beyond numbers, inspect where Genie fails: wrong aggregation grain, missing filter, wrong join — categorized failures guide what trusted assets to add next. Treat the eval set as a regression suite: every curation change should be gated by a run that either holds or improves all three metrics.
fj: Ask a few users if the answers looked right — that's enough validation.
fs: Genie benchmarking needs a labeled question set scored on SQL correctness, result accuracy, and coverage. Categorize failures to guide curation; run the set as a regression gate on every change.

followups.q: Why is SQL correctness harder to measure than result accuracy, and when does result accuracy mislead you?
followups.a: Two different SQL queries can produce the same result on current data but diverge on edge cases, historical periods, or after schema changes — so result accuracy passes even when the logic is wrong. SQL correctness checks the query structure, not just the output, catching wrong joins or filters that happen to produce the right number today. Use result accuracy for quick signal and SQL review for validation of critical business metrics.

followups.q: How do you build the ground-truth labeled set without it becoming a manual bottleneck?
followups.a: Start with the top 20-30 questions users actually ask (pull from Genie's conversation history once live, or interview business users). Have an analyst author the canonical SQL for each and verify the result. That set covers the high-frequency, high-stakes questions where errors cost the most. Grow it incrementally by adding any new failure type from production rather than trying to enumerate exhaustively up front.

followups.q: Genie's coverage metric is low — many questions get 'I can't answer that.' What do you investigate?
followups.a: Low coverage usually means vocabulary mismatch: Genie doesn't recognize the user's term for a column or metric because the UC descriptions use different language. Check whether the failing questions contain terms not in any column description, SQL expression label, or example query. Adding a SQL expression or description that uses the user's terminology typically unlocks that class of questions without any model changes.

q: What is the Genie instructions layer and what are the failure modes of over-relying on it?
a: Genie instructions are a free-text block inside a space where the curator writes natural-language directives that guide SQL generation — for example, 'always filter to completed orders unless the user explicitly asks for all statuses' or 'present revenue in thousands'. Genie includes these instructions in its LLM context alongside the question. The failure modes: (1) instructions are interpreted by the LLM and can be applied inconsistently across question phrasings; (2) as instructions grow long they compete for context and earlier rules can be crowded out; (3) a poorly worded instruction can interfere with unrelated questions; (4) there's no executable test for whether an instruction was applied. For anything with one correct definition, a SQL expression is strictly better. Instructions should be short, composable, and tested against an eval set just like code.
fj: Free-text instructions are the most powerful lever in a Genie space and should be the first thing you write.
fs: Instructions are the weakest trusted asset — LLM-interpreted, inconsistently applied, context-limited. Reserve them for soft editorial rules; encode every precise definition as a SQL expression.

followups.q: How do you test whether a specific Genie instruction is actually being followed?
followups.a: Write eval questions specifically designed to trigger the instruction and check the generated SQL. For example, if the instruction says 'always filter to completed orders', include questions about orders and verify the WHERE clause contains the status filter. This is the only way to confirm the instruction is applied consistently — reading the instruction text doesn't tell you whether the LLM honors it across phrasings.

followups.q: What's the maximum useful length for a Genie instructions block, and why?
followups.a: There's no hard limit, but beyond roughly 500-700 words you see diminishing returns and interference: long instruction sets fill context that could carry the question and schema, and later rules can override or dilute earlier ones depending on LLM attention. Keep each rule atomic and short. If you have 20 instructions, that's a signal the space is trying to do too much — split it into multiple focused Genie spaces instead.

followups.q: Give a concrete example where migrating an instruction to a SQL expression solved a reliability problem.
followups.a: Suppose the instruction says 'active means the user placed an order in the last 90 days.' Genie applies this inconsistently — sometimes it uses 89 days, sometimes it doesn't apply the filter on an ambiguous question. Replacing it with a SQL expression `is_active AS (last_order_date >= current_date - 90)` that Genie substitutes verbatim eliminates the variance: every query that triggers the term uses the exact predicate, testably and reproducibly.

q: Genie vs. a manually authored Databricks SQL dashboard: when does each win, and how do they complement each other?
a: A manually authored SQL dashboard wins when the question set is fixed and well-understood, when users need pixel-perfect layout and interactive filters, and when performance matters (queries are pre-tuned). Genie wins when users need ad-hoc exploration, when the question space is too broad to enumerate in dashboards, and when business users who can't write SQL need answers without a data engineer in the loop. They complement each other: a Genie space can surface a candidate SQL that an analyst then turns into a dashboard tile, and a dashboard can embed a Genie conversational widget for the questions that fall outside its fixed panels. Neither eliminates the other; the right pattern is a dashboard for the 20% of repeated questions and a Genie space for the long tail.
fj: Genie replaces the need for any SQL dashboards.
fs: Dashboards win for fixed, repeated, performance-tuned views; Genie wins for ad-hoc exploration and questions outside the enumerated set. Use both: dashboards for the 20% repeated common questions, Genie for the long tail.

followups.q: What's the strongest case against replacing a CFO's weekly revenue dashboard with a Genie space?
followups.a: The CFO's dashboard is pixel-perfect, pre-agreed, and its SQL has been audited for edge cases. Genie generates SQL dynamically, so if the question phrasing varies slightly, the aggregation might shift. For a board-level metric, reproducibility and auditability of the exact query matter more than flexibility. Keep the dashboard for the canonical view; Genie handles the follow-up questions.

followups.q: How can a Genie space accelerate building new dashboard tiles without replacing the dashboard?
followups.a: An analyst poses a question in Genie, inspects the generated SQL, refines it, and copies it into a SQL dashboard tile. Genie acts as a first-draft SQL generator that the analyst validates and owns — faster than authoring from scratch, but the dashboard tile is audited human-owned SQL, not a live Genie answer. This is the workflow Databricks recommends for analyst productivity.

followups.q: What governance property makes Genie safe to give to a business user, whereas raw SQL editor access is not?
followups.a: With SQL editor access a business user could author queries that scan expensive tables, bypass intended filters, or accidentally expose cross-tenant data if permissions are broad. Genie scopes the question to the tables in the space, runs as the user's identity so UC policies apply, and the space curator can set boundaries on what's answerable. Business users get analytics self-service within a curated, governed perimeter, not open-ended warehouse access.

q: How does the Genie space scoping model affect security, and what happens when a curator adds a table that contains more columns than the business question needs?
a: Every table in a Genie space is fair game for the LLM's SQL generation and for Unity Catalog permission checks — Genie can and will SELECT from any column in any attached table if it determines that column answers the question. Adding a table with sensitive columns (even ones not needed for the business use case) means those columns are in scope. The correct practice is to attach views or materialized columns that expose only the grain and attributes needed, so Genie's SQL generation space is constrained and overly broad SELECTs can't leak sensitive fields to users who shouldn't see them. Principle of least privilege applies to Genie space curation just as to direct data access.
fj: Attaching a full table to a Genie space is fine; Genie only uses the columns relevant to the question.
fs: Every column in an attached table is in scope for Genie's SQL. Attach narrow views not raw wide tables to enforce least-privilege and prevent accidental sensitive-column leakage.

followups.q: What UC object should you attach to a Genie space instead of the raw table when the table has 80 columns but only 10 are relevant?
followups.a: A Unity Catalog view that SELECTs only the 10 needed columns with clear aliases and a well-authored description. The view inherits the source table's row filters, is registered in UC with its own grants, and its column set constrains what Genie can SELECT. You can add computed columns like `is_active` into the view definition so Genie never needs to reconstruct that logic.

followups.q: If you attach two tables to a Genie space and both have a column named `id`, what problem does that cause and how do you fix it?
followups.a: Ambiguous column names cause Genie to either pick the wrong table's `id` in join conditions or generate unqualified SQL that errors. Fix it by attaching views that alias ambiguous columns to fully-qualified names (e.g. `customer_id`, `order_id`), and add a join relationship hint in trusted assets that shows the correct join key so Genie doesn't have to infer it.

followups.q: Can you restrict which rows of a table are visible in a Genie space without writing a separate view?
followups.a: Yes — UC row filters on the source table are enforced at scan time for any query Genie generates, including in the space. So if the table already has a row filter that limits rows by tenant or date range, Genie inherits it automatically. But if you need a Genie-specific subset (e.g. only finalized orders for a BI-facing space) and the table doesn't already have that filter, creating a view with a WHERE clause is the clean, explicit way to constrain the space.

q: What are Genie spaces' current limitations compared to a full SQL analytics workbench, and how do they affect senior architecture decisions?
a: As of early 2026 Genie spaces have several hard limits senior architects must plan around: (1) the underlying model has a context window that bounds how many tables, column descriptions, and examples fit — very wide schemas require narrowing via views; (2) Genie cannot run arbitrary Python or UDFs, only SQL over the attached tables; (3) multi-step reasoning (compute a temp result, then filter it) is expressed as subqueries in one shot, not as multi-hop tool calls like an agent; (4) it doesn't support write operations — no INSERT/UPDATE; (5) question history is conversational but not stateful across sessions by default. These limits mean Genie is the right answer for governed self-service analytics but not for operational workflows, ML feature pipelines, or complex multi-step data transformations — those still need notebooks or agents.
fj: Genie can replace all analytics tooling including Python notebooks and ML pipelines.
fs: Genie's limits: context window constrains schema breadth, SQL-only (no Python/UDFs), single-shot SQL (no multi-hop), read-only, no cross-session state. Design it for self-service BI, not operational or ML workloads.

followups.q: A finance team wants to use Genie to drive an automated month-end journal entry process. Why is that the wrong architecture?
followups.a: Genie is read-only NL-to-SQL — it can't execute INSERT, UPDATE, or procedural logic, and its SQL is generated non-deterministically from natural language, making it unsuitable as the engine for a controlled, auditable financial process. Month-end automation needs deterministic, tested SQL or dbt models, not LLM-generated SQL. Use Genie for ad-hoc exploration; use governed scheduled pipelines for operational writes.

followups.q: How do you architect around Genie's context window limit when the business domain has 200 relevant columns?
followups.a: Decompose into multiple focused Genie spaces, each covering a business domain with 15-30 well-described columns rather than one monolithic space. Users route their question to the right space (or a router layer directs them). Each space's context is dense and relevant, improving accuracy. Avoid forcing one space to cover everything — a bloated context hurts both accuracy and latency.

followups.q: When a user needs an answer that requires Python-level logic (e.g., a statistical model or a pandas transformation), what Databricks-native alternative covers the gap?
followups.a: A Mosaic AI Agent that has access to code execution (via a tool that calls a Databricks job or a notebook task) can handle Python-level logic while still routing simpler SQL questions through Genie via the Conversations API. The agent uses Genie for the SQL retrieval layer and falls back to Python execution for complex transformations. This is the correct architecture for a hybrid analytics assistant that needs both NL-to-SQL and computation.

q: How does Genie's SQL generation compare to Cortex Analyst's when the semantic model is mature, and what should drive the choice between them in a new Databricks-first org?
a: Both are mature NL-to-SQL systems backed by large models, so raw generation quality on well-curated models is comparable. The architectural difference is the semantic model artifact: Cortex Analyst's YAML is an explicit, versionable, CI-testable document that data engineers can diff and review in pull requests, while Genie's semantic layer is distributed across Unity Catalog comments, SQL expressions, and example queries — easier to author incrementally but harder to version atomically. In a Databricks-first org the decisive factors are (1) data is already in Delta under Unity Catalog — Genie inherits all UC governance automatically, no re-expression in YAML; (2) the audience is business users in the Databricks SQL UI — Genie is native and seamless; (3) if you want to embed conversational BI in a custom app, the Genie Conversations API gives programmatic access. Only choose Cortex Analyst if your data is primarily in Snowflake or if you need the YAML model to double as a dbt-style semantic contract.
fj: Cortex Analyst is always more accurate because it uses a structured YAML model.
fs: On mature models accuracy is comparable; choose Genie for Databricks-native UC governance flow-through and casual analyst UX, Cortex Analyst for Snowflake-first orgs or teams who need a CI-auditable YAML semantic contract.

followups.q: A team argues Cortex Analyst's YAML is better for governance because it's explicit and versionable. What's the Genie counter-argument?
followups.a: Genie's semantic layer is also version-controlled implicitly through Unity Catalog: column descriptions are table-level attributes that change with ALTER TABLE DDL (tracked via lineage), and SQL expressions are catalog objects. The difference is you can't PR-review a Genie space as a single diff the way you can a YAML file. The practical counter is that UC governance (row filters, masking, grants) flows into Genie for free; replicating that in a YAML semantic model requires hand-encoding what UC already enforces.

followups.q: If an org runs data on both Databricks and Snowflake, what's the recommended approach for conversational BI?
followups.a: Use each platform's native system on its own data: Genie spaces for Unity Catalog tables in Databricks, Cortex Analyst for Snowflake data. A routing layer in the front end or a unified agent dispatches questions to the appropriate system based on which platform owns the authoritative data for that domain. Avoid trying to federate one semantic model across both platforms — the governance models diverge and the maintenance burden exceeds the convenience.

followups.q: What does Genie's integration with Databricks AI/BI Dashboards give you that the Conversations API alone doesn't?
followups.a: The Databricks AI/BI Dashboard embeds a Genie conversational widget directly alongside static dashboard panels, so a user can ask a follow-up question about a dashboard chart and get a Genie-generated answer scoped to the same data without leaving the page. The Conversations API requires a custom app to wire this together.
