The native dashboard integration gives a no-code path to the hybrid fixed-plus-conversational BI pattern Databricks recommends, with no application development required.

q: You own the Mosaic AI platform for a 200-person data org. How do you architect the Model Serving layer to handle both online inference (sub-100 ms) and batch scoring (millions of rows/night) without doubling infrastructure?
a: Separate the serving topology by workload class rather than duplicating clusters. For online inference, provision dedicated endpoints sized to peak QPS with provisioned-throughput foundation models or custom model weights — autoscaling handles burst. For batch, route ai_query calls inside Databricks SQL or a Spark job against pay-per-token endpoints (or a provisioned endpoint that shares the same weight, but throttled during off-hours via gateway rate limits). The AI Gateway is the unification layer: one set of credentials and guardrails, logged to Unity Catalog so cost attribution is per-endpoint per-team. Avoid a 'one cluster for everything' approach — batch locks up online capacity during scoring windows. The staff-level insight is that the model weights can be the same (same Unity Catalog version), but the serving topology and rate contracts differ: dedicated+reserved for online, shared+throttled for batch.
fj: Run all inference on one large provisioned endpoint so it's simpler to manage.
fs: Separate topology: provisioned endpoints for latency-sensitive online traffic, pay-per-token or shared throttled endpoints for batch — same UC model version, different rate contracts. AI Gateway unifies governance.

followups.q: How do you prevent a runaway nightly batch job from consuming all provisioned throughput and spiking latency for online users?
followups.a: Apply endpoint-level rate limits in the AI Gateway for the batch caller's service principal, capping tokens-per-minute to a headroom-safe band below the endpoint's total capacity. The online callers hit the endpoint with no artificial ceiling; the batch job is throttled and retries with backoff. If the endpoint is shared, this isolation is the only thing protecting online SLOs — don't rely on scheduling alone, because overlap happens.

followups.q: How do you right-size a provisioned throughput endpoint, and what's the operational review cadence?
followups.a: Size from p95 online QPS multiplied by average tokens per call, with 20-30% headroom. Pull these numbers from AI Gateway usage logs in Unity Catalog — token-per-minute histograms per endpoint. Review monthly: downsize when p95 drops more than 25% below reserved capacity for two consecutive weeks, upsize when throttle errors appear. The review cadence matters because both over- and under-provisioning cost real money and SLOs.

followups.q: When does a model cascade (small model first, escalate to large) genuinely reduce cost at this scale, and when does it backfire?
followups.a: It pays when traffic is skewed — e.g. 70% of tickets are simple tier-1 queries the small model handles at 5x lower cost, and only 30% need the flagship model. At 200-person org scale with millions of nightly rows, even a 60% deflection to a cheaper model is meaningful. It backfires when the routing classifier itself is expensive, when the task distribution is uniformly hard (cascade doesn't deflect much), or when misroutes to the weak model produce costly remediation downstream. Always measure empirical deflection rate and error rate before committing the routing logic.

q: Your agent platform runs 15 distinct agents deployed via agents.deploy(). Explain how you govern prompt versions, model versions, and tool schemas across all of them as a Staff platform engineer.
a: Treat the agent definition as code: use MLflow Models-from-Code so each version is a source file committed to git with a deterministic hash, and register every agent as a versioned Unity Catalog model. Model versions track the LLM endpoint + Vector Search index at the time of logging as declared resources, so a deployed version is reproducible from its UC model version metadata. Gate promotions: agents move from dev alias to staging only after Agent Evaluation scores meet a defined quality bar on the eval set, then to prod via alias promotion rather than overwriting. For prompt governance, store prompt templates as MLflow Prompt Engineering artifacts or as parameterized strings in the agent source, versioned alongside the model — never inline ad-hoc strings in the serving environment. Tool schemas are versioned with the agent code; a breaking change to a tool contract increments the agent version. The cross-cutting risk is drift: an agent at Unity Catalog version 3 declares it needs serving endpoint X at a specific model, so a model upgrade on that endpoint must be coordinated with an agent re-evaluation cycle, not deployed silently.
fj: Deploy the latest prompt and model change directly to the production endpoint — agents are stateless, so rollback isn't needed.
fs: All three dimensions (prompt, model, tool schema) version together in the MLflow model source; UC alias promotion gates prod changes; Agent Evaluation is the quality gate. Drift between declared resource versions and live endpoint models is the main operational risk.

code.label: Promote agent alias to prod after eval
lines: # Gate: eval score must be >= threshold

followups.q: How do you handle a breaking tool schema change — e.g. a tool now requires an extra mandatory field — without taking down all 15 agents?
followups.a: Version the tool interface: introduce the new schema as a v2 tool and run both v1 and v2 side-by-side until all agents have been updated and re-evaluated. Agents that call the tool by name will keep working on v1; you migrate each agent in a controlled eval cycle and promote to prod only after passing quality gates. Avoid force-deprecating v1 until every agent's prod alias points to a version that uses v2.

followups.q: An agent in production is misbehaving after an unrelated model endpoint upgrade. How do your versioning practices let you diagnose and roll back quickly?
followups.a: Because the UC model version declares its required serving endpoint and the endpoint's model version at logging time, you can diff the current agent's declared resources against what's live. MLflow traces on the production endpoint show the exact model that generated each span. Roll back by moving the prod alias to the previous UC model version — agents.deploy() re-provisions the endpoint from that version's declared resources. Root cause is usually an undeclared dependency: the endpoint model was upgraded without re-evaluating and re-logging the agent.

followups.q: What's your strategy for keeping 15 agents' eval sets from becoming a maintenance burden?
followups.a: Factored eval sets: share a common base eval set for cross-cutting quality dimensions (groundedness, safety, format) across all agents, and maintain a small agent-specific addendum for domain-specific correctness. Run the base suite in CI for every agent build; run the agent-specific suite only for that agent's pipeline. Grow each addendum by routing production traces that receive low judge scores or user thumbs-down into a curation queue — evaluation stays tethered to real failure modes, not synthetic edge cases.

q: How do you design a cost and quality attribution system for Mosaic AI workloads across 10+ teams in a large org, and what does the monthly review cycle look like?
a: Unity Catalog is the data backbone: the AI Gateway logs every LLM request — endpoint, principal, tokens consumed, latency — into UC tables you own. Tag every serving endpoint with team metadata at creation time (endpoint name convention or custom tags), so log joins produce per-team, per-agent breakdowns in SQL. Build a daily Lakeflow pipeline that reads the gateway log, enriches with team ownership from an HR/catalog table, and writes to a governed cost_attribution Delta table. Layer on a Databricks SQL dashboard for the monthly review: total DBU + token cost per team, cost per 1K requests by agent, quality trend (average groundedness and correctness score from Agent Evaluation logs). Monthly review covers: (1) over-budget teams get provisioned throughput right-sized or model right-sized; (2) agents with degrading quality get an eval re-run and possible rollback; (3) batch workloads that ran on provisioned endpoints during online hours get flagged. The goal is making cost and quality first-class operational metrics, not after-the-fact billing surprises.
fj: Cost is the cloud provider's problem; teams self-manage their agents.
fs: AI Gateway logs → Lakeflow enrichment → governed cost_attribution table → SQL dashboard. Monthly review ties cost per team to quality trend and drives provisioning decisions. Make it a first-class operational metric.

code.label: Aggregate gateway usage by team
lines: PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY g.latency_ms) AS p50_latency

followups.q: A team's cost doubled month-over-month but their request count barely changed. What's your diagnostic path?
followups.a: Token count per request increased — look at the gateway log's per-request token breakdown. Common causes: a prompt change bloated context (retrieval returning more chunks, system prompt grew), a model upgrade that returns longer responses, or a new tool that injects large schemas into the prompt. Join the token spike to the deployment timeline — find when a new agent version went live. Then audit the prompt assembly for that agent: retrieve chunk count, context window fill rate.

followups.q: How do you prevent one team's batch job from appearing on another team's cost line if they share a serving endpoint?
followups.a: The AI Gateway logs the calling principal (service account or user identity), not the endpoint owner. As long as each team uses its own service principal for its workloads, the gateway log attribution is by caller, not by endpoint owner. Teams that share an endpoint still appear on separate lines because the caller identity differs. This is why each team needs its own service principal tied to its team in the catalog — shared service accounts break cost attribution.

followups.q: What's the right escalation path when a team's quality score (groundedness) is trending down over 4 weeks?
followups.a: First check whether a model endpoint upgrade or a data pipeline change correlates with the trend start. Pull the low-groundedness traces and have the team's SME review 20 examples to classify failure mode (retrieval returned wrong docs, model ignored context, chunking broke a key passage). If retrieval is the issue, reindex with better chunking and re-evaluate. If model behavior changed, re-evaluate the previous agent version against current traffic. The four-week trend is enough to distinguish noise from signal; don't roll back on one week of data.

q: An engineering team proposes deploying an open-source model (Llama 3.3 70B) on Databricks provisioned throughput instead of using Foundation Model APIs. Walk through the build-vs-buy tradeoff at Staff level.
a: Foundation Model APIs are the managed path: Databricks runs and patches the infrastructure, handles scale-out, and the cost is token-metered. They're the right default for most orgs. Hosting your own weights on provisioned throughput makes sense when: (1) you've fine-tuned the model on proprietary data and need to serve that custom checkpoint — FM APIs can't serve your weights; (2) your volume is high enough that the per-token rate of a managed endpoint is more expensive than the compute cost of running the model yourself at sustained QPS; (3) you need model configurability (quantization, context length, batch size) the managed offering doesn't expose; (4) data residency requirements mean model weights can't live on shared infrastructure. The hidden costs of self-hosting: you own GPU infra sizing, model patching cadence, serving software updates, and on-call for serving failures. At a 200-person org the default should be FM APIs unless one of the four triggers above is clearly met — the engineering leverage is usually better spent on agent quality than on model serving operations.
fj: Self-hosting is always cheaper because you avoid the per-token markup.
fs: FM APIs win for most orgs (managed infra, no ops). Self-host on provisioned throughput only for fine-tuned custom checkpoints, volume-driven economics, model config needs, or data residency — and price in the ops cost.

followups.q: How do you calculate the break-even point between FM API per-token pricing and provisioned throughput compute cost?
followups.a: Measure your sustained token throughput (tokens/hour averaged over a week). Multiply by FM API per-token rate to get the hourly API cost. Compare to the hourly DBU cost of the provisioned throughput cluster sized for that throughput. Break-even is where those two cross. Add 20% to the provisioned estimate for ops overhead (patching, alerting, on-call). In practice, break-even for a 70B model is typically well above 10M tokens/day of sustained load — most teams don't hit it.

followups.q: What model patching obligation do you take on when self-hosting a Llama checkpoint, and why does it matter for a production agent?
followups.a: Llama releases security patches and capability updates; Meta doesn't push them to you — you have to monitor, evaluate, and apply. A production agent that depends on the model's behavior needs re-evaluation every time the weights change, so a model patch triggers a full eval cycle before promotion. FM APIs handle this transparently (Databricks patches managed models); self-hosting means you own the upgrade runway, which can mean weeks of delay if your eval suite is slow.

followups.q: Your CISO says proprietary training data can't leave the Databricks workspace during inference. Does that change the analysis?
followups.a: It removes the external-provider option entirely — an FM API that proxies to a third-party model (Anthropic, OpenAI) sends request payloads off-workspace. Databricks-hosted FM API models (like Meta Llama on Foundation Model APIs) run inside the Databricks workspace, so inference stays in-boundary. Self-hosted provisioned throughput endpoints are also in-workspace. The data-residency requirement is not automatically an argument for self-hosting over FM APIs — check whether the specific Databricks-hosted FM API model is classified as workspace-resident in your data classification agreement.

q: How do you govern and operationalize an Agent Evaluation program across multiple squads shipping agents on Databricks, so quality regressions never reach production?
a: Three layers: eval-set ownership, automated CI gates, and production feedback loops. Each squad owns a versioned eval set registered as an MLflow dataset in Unity Catalog — questions, ground-truth answers, and metadata about when questions were added and by whom. CI (Databricks Asset Bundles or GitHub Actions calling the Databricks Jobs API) runs Agent Evaluation on every agent version candidate, comparing groundedness, correctness, and safety scores against the prior promoted version. A version can only be promoted to the staging alias if scores hold or improve; a regression on any judge blocks the promotion and requires a human review comment in the model version's UC annotation before override. Production feedback comes from two sources: the Review App (SME ratings) and gateway traces scored by the label-free groundedness judge in Lakehouse Monitoring. Low-score production traces automatically flow into a curation queue where the squad adds them to the eval set, closing the loop. The Staff-level pattern is treating eval sets as living governed datasets, not static test files — they grow from production failures and are reviewed in squad sprint ceremonies.
fj: Run the eval notebook manually before each release — that's sufficient quality control.
fs: Squad-owned UC eval datasets + CI gates on every version + production trace feedback loop → eval sets grow from real failures. Promotion requires passing all judges; regression blocks and requires human override annotation.

followups.q: A squad argues their agent is hard to evaluate because ground truth is subjective. How do you help them?
followups.a: Decompose quality into dimensions that can be evaluated without ground truth: groundedness (is the answer supported by context?), relevance (does the retrieval match the question?), and format compliance (does output conform to schema?) are all label-free. For correctness on subjective tasks, have two SMEs independently label a sample and measure inter-rater agreement — if they disagree on ground truth, that's a product scoping issue, not an eval problem. Resolve the definition of 'correct' with product leadership first; then the eval is straightforward.

followups.q: What's the risk of eval set contamination, and how do you prevent it?
followups.a: Contamination happens when questions from the eval set leak into the fine-tuning or retrieval corpus, causing artificially high scores. Prevent it by hashing eval question texts and running a similarity check against training data before each fine-tune job. Keep the eval set in a Unity Catalog table with write access restricted to the squad lead — not editable by the same engineer who tunes the model. Treat it like held-out test data in classical ML: the model is never trained on it.

followups.q: The label-free groundedness judge is flagging 15% of production traces as low-groundedness — what's your triage workflow?
followups.a: Sample 50 flagged traces and classify failure modes manually: (a) retrieval returned the wrong documents — fix is index or query improvement; (b) model was grounded but judge scored wrong — the judge needs calibration against human labels for this domain; (c) true hallucinations. For type (a) add the question to the eval set with the correct retrieval as the target. For type (b) tune the judge prompt and re-validate against labeled samples. For type (c) prioritize prompt hardening and consider adding an output guardrail in the AI Gateway. Don't treat '15% flagged' as one problem — it's at least three distinct root causes.

q: You are making the architectural decision on whether to add a dedicated vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate) alongside Mosaic AI Vector Search in the platform. What is the Staff-level decision framework?
a: Start from the governance and operational cost of running a second store. Mosaic AI Vector Search integrates into Unity Catalog — one permission model, one lineage graph, one sync mechanism (Delta Sync). Adding a second store immediately creates dual-write complexity, drift risk between the Delta source and the external index, and a parallel ACL system that must shadow Unity Catalog's row filters and masking. The legitimate triggers for a second store are: (1) a genuine capability gap — e.g. you need graph-based ANN, a specific HNSW variant, or multi-modal vectors Mosaic AI doesn't support as of early 2026; (2) cross-cloud portability where Vector Search isn't available; (3) existing high-value Pinecone investment that would cost more to migrate than to maintain. If none of those apply, the 'it's faster to demo' argument doesn't hold at platform scale — the ops cost of a second store is borne by every team that uses the platform, not just the team that added it. The decision is: capability gap first, then migration cost, then governance overhead. Default to staying native unless the gap is real.
fj: Add Pinecone alongside Vector Search — more options for teams is always better.
fs: Second vector stores multiply governance complexity and drift risk. Add one only for a genuine capability gap, cross-cloud need, or unmigratable investment. Capability gap first — not 'more options is better'.

followups.q: How do you assess whether Mosaic AI Vector Search's ANN performance is the actual bottleneck before concluding there's a capability gap?
followups.a: Benchmark it properly: measure recall@k vs. latency on your actual data distribution at your target QPS, with your chunk count, not on a toy dataset. Vector Search uses DiskANN-style approximate nearest neighbor; for most enterprise RAG corpora at <50M vectors and reasonable QPS, it's sufficient. Profile whether latency is dominated by embedding, retrieval, or generation — retrieval is rarely the bottleneck in RAG. Only if measured retrieval latency is the bottleneck AND optimizing index params (num_results, query parameters) doesn't help does a capability gap argument hold.

followups.q: A team wants to use a third-party vector DB for a PoC and promises to 'migrate later.' What's the governance conversation you need to have?
followups.a: The migration promise almost never materializes — PoCs become production. The governance conversation is: during the PoC, what access controls are on the external store? Does it honor UC row filters? What happens to PII that gets embedded there? How are credentials managed? If the answers are 'we'll figure it out later,' the PoC is building ungoverned infrastructure. The staff-level intervention is either requiring a governance plan before the PoC starts, or insisting the PoC use Vector Search from day one so the 'migration' is already done.

followups.q: How does multi-modal retrieval (images + text in the same index) change the analysis in early 2026?
followups.a: As of early 2026, Mosaic AI Vector Search supports text and dense numeric vectors but does not offer native multi-modal index support for arbitrary image embeddings alongside text in the same index. If an application genuinely needs image+text co-retrieval (e.g. a product catalog search over images and descriptions simultaneously), that is a real capability gap. The correct architecture is to either pre-compute multi-modal embeddings externally and store them in Vector Search as dense numeric vectors if the format is compatible, or to maintain a separate specialized store only for the multi-modal use case while keeping all text RAG in Vector Search — not replacing Vector Search entirely.

q: How would you architect Mosaic AI's rollout across a newly acquired company whose ML stack is entirely SageMaker + Bedrock, including the migration sequencing and what you leave in place?
a: Start with inventory and don't migrate everything. Categorize the SageMaker estate by workload type: (a) online inference endpoints; (b) batch transform jobs; (c) training pipelines; (d) feature pipelines. Most orgs discover that 60-70% of SageMaker endpoints serve models that are rarely updated and low-traffic — those are migration candidates. Begin with governance: register existing SageMaker model artifacts as external model versions in Unity Catalog so you have lineage without yet moving serving. Next, migrate serving by standing up equivalent Mosaic AI endpoints (provisioned throughput for custom weights, FM APIs for commodity models) and running shadow traffic before cutover. Third, migrate features: re-express SageMaker Feature Store features as Databricks Feature Engineering feature specs, using Lakebase as the online store. Bedrock-backed agents are the trickiest: register the Bedrock models as external models in the AI Gateway so traffic flows through Databricks governance while you build equivalent Mosaic AI agents, then cut over. Leave in place any SageMaker capability with no equivalent — ground-truth labeling (Label Studio/SageMaker Ground Truth) or specialized SageMaker Studio notebooks the team depends on — and integrate via Delta Sharing or API rather than forklift. Sequence: governance registration first, serving migration second, feature migration third, training pipelines last (highest risk, lowest governance payoff).
fj: Migrate everything to Mosaic AI at once — unified platform is the goal.
fs: Inventory → govern in place (UC registration) → migrate serving → features → training pipelines. Leave true capability gaps (labeling tools) integrated, not forklift-migrated. Governance first, compute last.

followups.q: How do you handle models that the acquired team fine-tuned on SageMaker and actively retrain weekly?
followups.a: Active weekly retraining pipelines are high-risk to migrate cold. First, port the training data pipeline to Delta (establish the data layer), register the existing SageMaker model artifacts in Unity Catalog as versions with provenance metadata so lineage is tracked. Then run a parallel Mosaic AI Model Training run on the same data and compare eval metrics before switching. Only cut over the retraining pipeline once both runs produce equivalent quality for three consecutive weeks. Never cut over training and serving simultaneously — decouple them.

followups.q: The acquired team uses Bedrock for a customer-facing chatbot with 5M monthly users. What's the cutover risk, and how do you mitigate it?
followups.a: Customer-facing means zero-tolerance for latency regression or capability degradation. Cutover risk is: new endpoint doesn't handle the QPS, latency profile changes, or the replacement model behaves differently enough to degrade user experience. Mitigate with shadow traffic for four weeks (route 5% to Mosaic AI, 95% stays on Bedrock), measuring latency p99, error rate, and conversation quality scores side by side. Agree a rollback trigger with product leadership — if p99 latency on Mosaic AI exceeds Bedrock by >30ms, pause. Promote only after shadow traffic shows equivalent quality at full QPS.

followups.q: What's the argument for leaving the acquired team's Bedrock models as external models in the AI Gateway rather than migrating them to Databricks-hosted models?
followups.a: External model registration is the lowest-risk, lowest-cost integration: the Bedrock model stays on Bedrock, but all traffic flows through the AI Gateway for unified rate limits, logging, and guardrails. The acquired team gets governance continuity without a model migration. The migration to Databricks-hosted or fine-tuned models is a separate, later decision driven by cost optimization or capability needs — not a prerequisite for governance. Forcing a model migration to justify Databricks adoption is the common mistake; governance can precede compute consolidation.

q: As a Principal, define the multi-year strategic bet: should a large enterprise standardize on Mosaic AI as its unified GenAI-plus-ML stack, or maintain a best-of-breed stack (separate vector DB, MLflow self-hosted, external LLM provider APIs)?
a: The standardization bet is fundamentally a governance bet, not a capability bet. As of early 2026 Mosaic AI's individual components — Vector Search, Model Serving, Agent Evaluation, Feature Engineering — are production-grade but not universally best-in-class on every dimension. The strategic case for standardization: (1) governance compound returns — Unity Catalog governance accretes value as more assets register, because lineage, masking, and ACLs span the whole estate automatically, reducing audit burden and data-governance headcount; (2) platform leverage — teams build on shared primitives (shared serving endpoints, shared eval infrastructure, shared gateway guardrails) instead of each team owning a bespoke stack; (3) data gravity — LLM workloads that operate on data already in Delta under Unity Catalog pay zero egress tax and inherit access controls, while best-of-breed stacks pay continuous sync and governance reconciliation cost. The counter-case: vendor lock-in concentrates platform risk, best-of-breed components may have 2-3x better performance or cost in specific dimensions (e.g. Pinecone's ANN performance, Anthropic's frontier model quality), and Databricks' roadmap is a bet on their execution. The principal-level synthesis: standardize the governance layer (Unity Catalog, AI Gateway) unconditionally — it's the moat, and the lock-in risk is low because UC assets are Delta/MLflow, which are open. Standardize the serving and evaluation layer for all net-new workloads. Leave escape hatches via external models and external Vector Search only where capability gaps are documented and reviewed annually. Sunset escape hatches when Mosaic AI closes the gap.
fj: Best-of-breed is always safer because you're not locked into one vendor.
fs: Standardize governance unconditionally (UC, AI Gateway — low lock-in risk, compounding value). Standardize serving/eval for net-new. External escape hatches only for documented capability gaps, reviewed annually. The moat is governance, not compute.

followups.q: How do you define and track the 'documented capability gap' escape hatch annually so it doesn't become a permanent exemption?
followups.a: Each escape hatch is registered: the capability claimed to be missing, the external tool filling it, the team, and an annual review date. At review, the platform team benchmarks whether Mosaic AI has closed the gap in the past year (Databricks ships fast; Vector Search gained hybrid and multi-modal improvements each half). If the gap is closed, the team gets a migration timeline — 6 months to move to native. If not closed, the exemption renews. Tracking in a Unity Catalog table with the review date as a partition makes the audit trivial and visible to leadership.

followups.q: A top ML team says they want to use a separate MLflow self-hosted instance to avoid 'Databricks dependency' on experiment tracking. How do you respond at the principal level?
followups.a: MLflow is open-source and the protocol is standard — there is no meaningful lock-in at the experiment-tracking layer. The question is operational: a self-hosted MLflow means the team runs infra (S3 artifact store, database, auth). The stronger argument is lineage: Databricks-managed MLflow participates in Unity Catalog lineage, so a model registered there carries its training-data provenance into governance workflows. Self-hosted breaks that link. The principal-level conversation is: the 'dependency' concern is about the serving and serving-to-data integration, not experiment tracking. Accept self-hosted MLflow only if the team is also willing to maintain the lineage bridge to UC, and make that a condition of the exemption.

followups.q: How do you model the governance compounding return to justify the standardization investment to a CFO skeptical of platform lock-in?
followups.a: Governance cost is roughly linear in the number of data+AI asset types you govern, multiplied by the number of governance frameworks in play. Best-of-breed with five tools means up to five ACL systems, five audit logs, and five lineage graphs to reconcile — staff time to maintain that scales with org size. Unity Catalog collapses it to one. Model it as: each additional governed asset type on UC costs near-zero incremental governance overhead, while each additional external tool adds N engineer-days/year of reconciliation and audit prep. Quantify the current reconciliation cost (how long does a compliance audit take?), project it forward with the headcount plan, and compare to the platform investment. The CFO case is not 'best platform' but 'lowest total governance cost as the estate grows.' Lock-in risk is mitigated by UC being built on open formats (Delta, Parquet, MLflow) — data can be extracted without reformatting.

q: As a Principal, how do you set the org-wide GenAI governance standards for Mosaic AI deployments — covering model risk tiering, evaluation thresholds, and production monitoring requirements — and how do you enforce them without becoming a bottleneck?
a: Start with a risk tiering framework, not a one-size policy. Tier 1: GenAI features that affect regulated decisions (credit, employment, health) — highest bar, mandatory human-in-the-loop, external audit, monthly model review. Tier 2: customer-facing features affecting brand or customer trust (chatbots, personalized recommendations) — Agent Evaluation correctness and groundedness thresholds published centrally, mandatory Review App sign-off by a product owner before prod alias promotion, weekly Lakehouse Monitoring review. Tier 3: internal productivity tools (code assistants, internal Q&A agents) — evaluation thresholds lower, self-certified by the team, monthly sampling review. The standards define minimum thresholds (e.g. groundedness ≥ 0.85 for Tier 2) and required monitoring (groundedness and latency alert on every Tier 1/2 endpoint). Enforcement without bottleneck: encode the standards in the CI/CD pipeline using Asset Bundles — a bundle validator checks that every endpoint config has monitoring enabled, that the eval score artifact meets the tier threshold, and that the model version has a UC annotation for the tier classification. The pipeline fails if standards aren't met, not a committee. The governance office (you) owns the standards file and the CI validator, not every deployment decision. Annually review tier definitions as product and regulatory landscape shifts.
fj: All GenAI deployments should go through a central governance review board before shipping.
fs: Risk-tiered standards (3 tiers by regulatory and customer impact) + eval thresholds encoded in CI validators, not review boards. The Principal owns the standard; the pipeline enforces it. Governance scales with the org, not with your calendar.

followups.q: A team deploys a Tier 2 customer-facing agent and argues their use case is 'just a search feature' to avoid the Review App sign-off requirement. How do you handle it?
followups.a: The tier classification is determined by the impact surface, not the feature label. A 'search feature' that returns AI-generated summaries to customers, could surface harmful or incorrect information, or influences a customer decision is Tier 2 by definition. The CI validator checks the tier annotation on the UC model version — if the team classified it as Tier 3 to skip the requirement, that's a miscategorization, not a policy exception. Walk them through the classification criteria; if there's genuine ambiguity on the boundary, the principal call is to err Tier 2 and use the Review App as a lightweight two-hour exercise, not a multi-week gate.

followups.q: How do evaluation thresholds interact with model upgrades — if Databricks upgrades a Foundation Model API model, do all Tier 2 agents automatically re-enter the eval gate?
followups.a: Yes, and this should be an automated trigger, not a manual process. The CI/CD system monitors for model version changes on declared endpoint resources. When a FM API model version changes, a Databricks workflow automatically re-runs Agent Evaluation for all agents that declare that endpoint as a resource, and posts the result to the UC model version annotation. If scores drop below threshold, the prod alias is frozen and the owning team is paged. This is why declaring resources at log time matters — it creates the dependency graph that the monitoring can traverse. Without declared resources, you can't know which agents are affected by a model change.

followups.q: Regulatory requirements in your industry require explainability for any AI decision. How does Mosaic AI's architecture support or limit that requirement for Tier 1 agents?
followups.a: MLflow Tracing captures the full reasoning trace — retrieved documents, tool calls, model inputs/outputs — per request, which is the closest approximation to explainability for LLM agents: you can reconstruct what information the model had and what it generated. Store traces for Tier 1 agents with a longer retention policy in a governed Unity Catalog table. For regulated decisions that require deeper explainability, pair the agent trace with SHAP or attention-based analysis on a classification model that makes the final decision, keeping the LLM as a reasoning aid rather than the decision-maker. The architecture limitation is that transformer attention is not yet auditor-accepted as causal explainability — the trace is evidence of process, not a feature-importance explanation. Document this distinction in your governance standards and design Tier 1 flows so the human-in-the-loop step is the explainable decision point.

q: You are advising a 500-person enterprise deciding between building their ML/AI platform on Mosaic AI vs. maintaining a separate ML stack (Kubeflow + self-hosted MLflow + Pinecone + direct OpenAI). Make the multi-year total-cost-of-ownership argument.
a: Total cost of ownership has four components: direct infrastructure cost, governance and compliance cost, platform engineering headcount, and opportunity cost of velocity. On direct infra: Mosaic AI is bundled into Databricks DBU pricing, so the marginal cost of Model Serving, Vector Search, and Feature Engineering is incremental on an existing Databricks contract — compare to the Kubernetes cluster for Kubeflow (always-on GPU nodes), Pinecone's per-pod or storage-unit pricing, and OpenAI's per-token rate (which the AI Gateway can surface on any provider). On governance: a self-managed stack requires a dedicated platform engineering team to maintain UC equivalents — an internal model registry, a vector DB ACL system, a usage logging pipeline, and a compliance dashboard. At 500 people that's typically 3-5 FTEs. Unity Catalog as a managed service replaces most of that with configuration. On platform engineering: Kubeflow's operational surface (upgrades, pod scheduling, node pools, GPU drivers) requires deep K8s expertise; Mosaic AI's serverless and provisioned endpoints are managed. At 500 people, the Kubeflow team is typically 4-6 engineers; Mosaic AI reduces that to 2-3 who focus on configuration and eval frameworks. Opportunity cost: teams that don't own platform infra ship features faster. The three-year TCO model usually shows the self-managed stack is cheaper on compute alone but 40-60% more expensive in total when governance and platform headcount are included. The principal-level recommendation: if the org is already on Databricks, the incremental cost of Mosaic AI is small and the governance dividend is immediate. If the org is Databricks-first for data but considering a separate ML stack to 'avoid lock-in,' the lock-in risk on open-format Delta + MLflow is low, and the governance consolidation savings outweigh it within 18 months.
fj: Self-managed Kubeflow + Pinecone is cheaper because you avoid vendor per-token markups.
fs: Direct compute cost favors self-managed; total cost including governance headcount (3-5 FTE) and platform ops (4-6 FTE) favors Mosaic AI by 40-60% over 3 years for an org already on Databricks. Lock-in risk is low on open formats.

followups.q: The self-managed team argues they can use open-source tooling and avoid all vendor costs. What's the hidden cost they're not modeling?
followups.a: Open-source is not free to operate. Hidden costs: (1) upgrade treadmill — Kubeflow, Pinecone OSS, and self-hosted MLflow each have their own release cycle; at 500 people a major version upgrade is a multi-sprint project; (2) security patching — CVEs in ML serving libraries need immediate response, which is a dedicated job; (3) GPU driver compatibility — keeping ML libraries, CUDA versions, and K8s driver plugins aligned is a full-time job at scale; (4) on-call — every component the team operates is a page at 2am. These costs are real and recurring; model them explicitly in the TCO rather than assuming 'open-source = $0 ops'.

followups.q: How does the TCO argument change if the enterprise is not already on Databricks and would be greenfielding?
followups.a: Greenfield changes the calculus: the org pays full Databricks platform cost (not incremental), so the direct cost comparison tightens. The governance argument still holds but the full platform cost must be weighed against the combined cost of Snowflake/BigQuery for data + SageMaker/Kubeflow for ML. At greenfield, the principal recommendation shifts to a build-vs-buy evaluation across the full data+AI stack rather than a narrow Mosaic AI vs. self-managed ML comparison. If the org needs a modern lakehouse anyway, Databricks' bundled pricing often still wins on three-year TCO; if the org is already on a cloud-native data warehouse, the incremental cost of adding Mosaic AI to Databricks may not justify a platform migration.

followups.q: What's the migration exit cost if the enterprise adopts Mosaic AI and then needs to move off in year 4?
followups.a: This is the lock-in risk to quantify explicitly. Models in Unity Catalog are stored as MLflow model artifacts on cloud object storage in open format — they're portable. Delta tables are Parquet under the hood — portable. Vector Search indexes are not directly portable (proprietary ANN index format), but the source Delta table is, and you re-embed on a new platform. The gateway config and eval datasets are the least portable, but they're configuration and labeled data — re-implementable. The exit cost is dominated by: (1) re-building the governance layer equivalent (the biggest sunk cost); (2) re-indexing vectors; (3) re-wiring CI/CD. Estimate 6-12 months of platform engineering effort at a 500-person org. That's real but not catastrophic, and much lower than a database migration. Communicate this as a manageable exit cost, not a trap.
